overview: Converts the character set encoding of an input document and optionally updates encoding declarations in XML and HTML files. The step can un-escape various character reference notations (NCR, CER, Java-style) on input, and re-escape characters using configurable notation on output. It automatically detects and updates XML encoding declarations and HTML charset declarations in the first 1024 characters of each document.

unescapeNCR.help:
Un-escapes all types of **numeric character references** (NCRs) when reading the input document. Converts decimal and hexadecimal forms to their actual characters.

- `ÿ` → `ÿ`
- `á` → `á`
- `&#e1;` → `á`

If disabled, NCRs in the input remain in their original form in the output.

unescapeCER.help:
Un-escapes standard **HTML character entity references** (CERs) when reading the input document. Converts named entities to their actual characters.

The five XML-reserved entities are **never** un-escaped regardless of this setting:
- `&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`

These are preserved because they may need to remain escaped in HTML and XML documents.
notes: The entities `&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, and `"` are never un-escaped, as they may need to be preserved regardless of encoding in HTML and XML documents.

unescapeJava.help:
Un-escapes **Java-style** `\uXXXX` escape sequences when reading the input document. Both uppercase and lowercase hex digits are recognized.

If disabled, Java-style escapes remain in their original form in the output.

escapeAll.help:
Controls which characters are escaped in the output:

- **false** — Only characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped
- **true** — All extended characters (non-ASCII) are escaped, regardless of whether the output encoding supports them

escapeNotation.help:
Selects the escape notation used when characters must be escaped in the output.

| Value | Notation | Example (`á`) |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| 0 | Uppercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 1 | Lowercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 2 | Decimal NCR | `ÿ` |
| 3 | Character entity reference | `á` |
| 4 | Uppercase Java-style | `\u00E1` |
| 5 | Lowercase Java-style | `\u00e1` |
| 6 | User-defined notation | Uses `userFormat` |

When using CER notation (3), if no entity is defined for a character, uppercase hex NCR is used as fallback.
escapeNotation.values: Integer `0`–`6` selecting the escape notation. `0` = uppercase hex NCR, `1` = lowercase hex NCR, `2` = decimal NCR, `3` = CER (with hex NCR fallback), `4` = uppercase Java, `5` = lowercase Java, `6` = user-defined format.
notes: When set to `3` (CER), characters without a corresponding entity definition fall back to uppercase hexadecimal NCR form.

userFormat.help:
A **Java format expression** that takes a single integer argument, used when `escapeNotation` is set to `6` (user-defined). The integer value is the Unicode code point of the character being escaped (or the byte values if `useBytes` is enabled).

Any valid `String.format()` pattern accepting one integer works. Use `%d` for decimal, `%x` for lowercase hex, `%X` for uppercase hex.
userFormat.values: Java `String.format()` expression with one integer placeholder. Common specifiers: `%d` (decimal), `%x` (lowercase hex), `%X` (uppercase hex).

dependsOn.condition: must be 6 (user-defined) for this to take effect
when true, values are byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points

useBytes.help:
Changes the values passed to the `userFormat` expression from **Unicode code points** to **byte values** of the output encoding.

This is useful for encodings like Shift-JIS where multi-byte sequences need to be expressed as individual byte escapes rather than single code point values.

dependsOn.condition: must be 6 (user-defined) for this to take effect
the format expression that receives the byte values

examples: With format `\'%x` and Shift-JIS output: `日本語` → `\'93\'fa\'96\'7b\'8c\'ea` (byte values) instead of `\'65e5\'672c\'8a9e` (code points)

BOMonUTF8.help:
Adds a **Byte-Order-Mark** (U+FEFF) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files. The BOM is a zero-width no-break space that some applications use to identify the file as UTF-8.

Only applies when the output encoding is UTF-8.
notes: The BOM is only relevant for UTF-8 output. Some applications (notably older Windows programs) expect a BOM to identify UTF-8 files, while others may display it as an unwanted character.

reportUnsupported.help: Logs all characters that cannot be represented in the output encoding. Each unsupported character is listed in the pipeline log, helping identify potential data loss during encoding conversion.

unescapeNCR.title: Unescape Numeric Character References
unescapeNCR.description: Un-escape numeric character references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.

unescapeCER.title: Unescape Character Entity References
unescapeCER.description: Un-escape HTML character entity references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.

unescapeJava.title: Unescape Java-style Notation
unescapeJava.description: Un-escape Java-style \uXXXX escape sequences when reading the input document.

escapeAll.title: Escape All Extended Characters
escapeAll.description: Escape all extended (non-ASCII) characters, not just those unsupported by the output encoding.

escapeNotation.title: Escape Notation
escapeNotation.description: Select the notation format used when escaping characters in the output (NCR, CER, Java-style, or user-defined).

userFormat.title: User-Defined Format
userFormat.description: Java format expression (e.g., %d, %x) used for escaping when user-defined notation is selected.

useBytes.title: Use Byte Values
useBytes.description: Use byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points in the user-defined format.

BOMonUTF8.title: BOM on UTF-8 Output
BOMonUTF8.description: Add a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files.

reportUnsupported.title: Report Unsupported Characters
reportUnsupported.description: Log all characters not supported by the output encoding.

processingNotes: Encoding declarations are detected and updated by scanning the **first 1024 characters** of each document — the routine is not XML/HTML-aware and cannot distinguish between normal code and comments.
XML documents are detected by encoding declarations, XML declarations, or `.xml` file extension — missing declarations may be inserted or added.
HTML documents are detected by charset declarations or file extensions starting with `.htm` — missing charset declarations may be inserted after `<head>` or `<html>`.
A document can be both XML and HTML and have both types of encoding/charset declarations updated.
limitations: Encoding declaration detection operates on raw text (first 1024 chars) and is not XML/HTML-aware — it cannot distinguish declarations inside comments from real ones.
HTML charset insertion requires a `<head>` element or at minimum an `<html` string to anchor the insertion point.
The five XML-reserved character entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`) are never un-escaped by the CER option.

examples.title: User-defined escape for Shift-JIS byte values
examples.description: Using a custom format expression with byte values to produce Shift-JIS byte-level escapes for CJK characters.
examples.config:
```yaml
escapeNotation: 6
userFormat: "'\'%x'"
useBytes: true
# Output encoding: Shift-JIS
```

examples.title: User-defined escape with decimal brackets
examples.description: Using a bracketed decimal format for characters unsupported by the output encoding.

fullDoc:
# Encoding Conversion Step

Converts the character set encoding of an input document and optionally updates encoding declarations in XML and HTML files. The step can un-escape various character reference notations (NCR, CER, Java-style) on input, and re-escape characters using configurable notation on output. It automatically detects and updates XML encoding declarations and HTML charset declarations in the first 1024 characters of each document.

**Takes:** Raw document. **Sends:** Raw document.

## Encoding Declaration Updates

The step scans the first 1024 characters of each document to detect and update encoding declarations. Note that this scan is not XML/HTML-aware and cannot distinguish between declarations inside comments and real ones.

### XML Documents

- If an XML encoding declaration is found → the encoding value is updated
- If an XML declaration exists without encoding → an encoding declaration is inserted
- If the file has a `.xml` extension but no XML declaration → one is added at the top

### HTML Documents

- If an HTML charset declaration is found → the charset value is updated
- If the file extension starts with `.htm` and no charset exists:
  - If `<head>` is found → a charset declaration is added after it
  - Otherwise if `<html` is found → `<head>` and charset are added after the opening tag

A document can be both XML and HTML and have both types of declarations.

## Parameters

### Input

#### Unescape Numeric Character References

Un-escapes all types of numeric character references (NCRs) when reading the input. Converts decimal (`ÿ`), uppercase hex (`á`), and lowercase hex (`&#e1;`) forms to their actual characters. If disabled, NCRs pass through unchanged.

#### Unescape Character Entity References

Un-escapes standard HTML character entity references (CERs) when reading the input (e.g., `á` → `á`).

> **Note:** The five XML-reserved entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`) are never un-escaped, as they may need to be preserved regardless of encoding.

#### Unescape Java-style Notation

Un-escapes Java-style `\uXXXX` escape sequences when reading the input. Both uppercase and lowercase hex digits are recognized (e.g., `\u00e1` and `\u00E1` both become `á`).

### Output

#### Escape All Extended Characters

When enabled, all extended (non-ASCII) characters are escaped in the output. When disabled, only characters unsupported by the output encoding are escaped.

#### Escape Notation

Selects the notation format for escaping characters:

| Value | Notation | Example (`á`) |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| 0 | Uppercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 1 | Lowercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 2 | Decimal NCR | `ÿ` |
| 3 | Character entity reference | `á` |
| 4 | Uppercase Java-style | `\u00E1` |
| 5 | Lowercase Java-style | `\u00e1` |
| 6 | User-defined notation | Uses User-Defined Format |

When using CER (3), characters without a defined entity fall back to uppercase hex NCR.

#### User-Defined Format

A Java `String.format()` expression with one integer placeholder, used when Escape Notation is set to 6. The integer is the Unicode code point (or byte value if Use Byte Values is enabled). Examples: `[[%d]]` produces `[[255]]` for `ÿ`; `\'%x` produces `\'65e5\'672c\'8a9e` for `日本語`.

#### Use Byte Values

Changes the values passed to the user-defined format expression from Unicode code points to byte values of the output encoding. Useful for multi-byte encodings like Shift-JIS where individual bytes need to be escaped separately.

#### BOM on UTF-8 Output

Adds a Byte-Order-Mark (U+FEFF) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files. Only relevant when the output encoding is UTF-8. Some applications expect a BOM to identify UTF-8 files, while others may display it as an unwanted character.

#### Report Unsupported Characters

Logs all characters not supported by the output encoding to the pipeline log, helping identify potential data loss.

## Limitations

- Encoding declaration detection operates on raw text (first 1024 chars) and is not XML/HTML-aware — it cannot distinguish declarations inside comments from real ones.
- HTML charset insertion requires a `<head>` element or at minimum an `<html` string to anchor the insertion point.
- The five XML-reserved character entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`) are never un-escaped by the CER option.

## Notes

- A document can be both XML and HTML and have both types of encoding/charset declarations updated.
- The step processes raw documents — it operates before or after filter-based extraction, not on segmented content.

## Examples

### User-defined escape for Shift-JIS

```yaml
escapeNotation: 6
userFormat: "\'%x"
useBytes: true
# Output encoding: Shift-JIS
```

Input: `日本語` → Output: `\'93\'fa\'96\'7b\'8c\'ea` (Shift-JIS byte values)

### Bracketed decimal format

```yaml
escapeNotation: 6
userFormat: "[[%d]]"
useBytes: false
```

Input: `á` → Output: `[[225]]` (Unicode code point in decimal)
